title: The Debauched Hospodar
description: The Debauched Hospodar is a pornographic novel by Guillaume Apollinaire. Ruled indecent in 1965 unless circulation was "restricted to persons professionally engaged in the study of abnormal psychology".
descriptionParagraphs: The Debauched Hospodar is a pornographic novel by Guillaume Apollinaire. Ruled indecent in 1965 unless circulation was "restricted to persons professionally engaged in the study of abnormal psychology".

Its interest lies partly in the way literary or informational writing gets collapsed into a public-morality problem. As a pornographic novel, it can be read not only for subject matter but for the way form, tone, and circulation make a text feel dangerous, intimate, or politically usable to anxious officials.

It also matters as part of a wider censorship history in New Zealand. The present page is a dossier starter built from source-tracked ban records; the surviving note currently says: Ruled indecent in 1965 unless circulation was "restricted to persons professionally engaged in the study of abnormal psychology". Given an R18 classification in 1975. More publication history, translations, and close reading can be added later.

overview.englishDescription: The Debauched Hospodar is a pornographic novel by Guillaume Apollinaire. Ruled indecent in 1965 unless circulation was "restricted to persons professionally engaged in the study of abnormal psychology".
banningParagraphs: The Debauched Hospodar entered censorship debates as a pornographic novel associated with morality, print scandal, and sexuality. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around obscenity and public morality.

The earliest event currently captured here is 20th century in New Zealand, where Indecent Publications Tribunal classified, prohibited, or restricted. Ruled indecent in 1965 unless circulation was "restricted to persons professionally engaged in the study of abnormal psychology". Given an R18 classification in 1975. counterReadings.title: Banned Books: Literature Suppressed on Sexual Grounds
counterReadings.author: Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: Surveys the legal and moral language used to suppress books as obscene.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for seeing how obscenity law and censorship habits changed over time.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.
